zones.name: CLI End-to-End Test Suite
zones.description: End-to-end integration tests that exercise the full CLI surface — init, analyze, import, prune, sync, recommend, and adapter flows — alongside the schema validation module.
insights: Low cohesion (0.29) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
High coupling (0.71) — 1 imports target "prd-analysis-engine"
The co-location of src/schema/validate.ts with e2e tests is unusual; validate.ts would be better placed in the store or core zone so it is accessible without coupling through the test layer.
Low cohesion (0.29) is expected for an e2e test zone since tests intentionally reach across all zones — this is not a structural concern.

Coverage spans 8+ CLI commands (cli-adapter, cli-analyze, cli-import, cli-init, cli-prune, cli-quiet, cli-recommend, and more), indicating good breadth; ensure slow e2e tests are tagged to avoid blocking fast unit feedback loops.
src/schema/validate.ts is grouped with e2e tests likely due to import patterns; relocating it to the store or core zone boundary would improve discoverability and reduce accidental e2e coupling.
Broad CLI command coverage across 16 files with dedicated per-command test files is a strong quality signal for the CLI interface contract.

src/schema/validate.ts is a production source file classified inside a test zone solely due to import proximity; this means the schema validator has no stable importable path from production code, forcing callers to reach through the test layer boundary.

src/schema/validate.ts is a production artifact stranded in a test zone — any production code importing it creates a source→test dependency, which is an inversion of the normal dependency direction and will break tree-shaking and test isolation.

Call-graph cohesion of 1.0 with 0 outgoing calls confirms cli-e2e-test-suite is a true black-box test zone with no direct function-call coupling to implementation zones. The import-cohesion score of 0.29 reflects test harness and fixture imports, not production coupling — this zone's import metric should be excluded from production architectural health calculations entirely.

The call-graph showing zero outgoing calls from the zone that contains src/schema/validate.ts confirms the schema validator has zero production call-site usage visible to the analyzer — it is only exercised through the test boundary, making it effectively invisible to any static dependency analysis that production code relies on.

src/schema/validate.ts is a production source file with zero outgoing call-graph edges from within its host zone, confirming it has no production callers. Any production module that imports it must traverse a source→test zone dependency, which inverts normal dependency direction, breaks tree-shaking, and will cause validate.ts to be excluded from any production bundle analysis or dead-code elimination pass. This file must be relocated to the store or core zone before any build optimization work is attempted.

The zone name 'cli-e2e-test-suite' implies test-only content, yet it contains src/schema/validate.ts, a production source file. No CI check or linter appears to enforce the invariant that files under src/ cannot be classified into test zones. The absence of such enforcement means this misclassification has persisted undetected, indicating the classification boundary is a convention with no mechanical enforcement.

Call-graph cohesion of 1.0 with zero outgoing edges confirms the e2e zone is a black-box boundary, but src/schema/validate.ts breaks this guarantee: it is a production file with a call signature accessible from outside the test boundary. A caller importing validate.ts from production code creates a src→test dependency not visible in the call graph, which means call-graph cohesion of 1.0 is a false positive for this zone — the actual production coupling is hidden.

Add a CI lint rule that fails if any file matching src/**/* is classified by zone analysis into a zone whose ID contains 'test', 'e2e', or 'spec'. This mechanically prevents recurrence of the src/schema/validate.ts misclassification without requiring manual zone audits.

The call-graph cohesion of 1.0 for cli-e2e-test-suite is a false positive: it reflects zero outgoing calls FROM the zone, but src/schema/validate.ts inside the zone CAN be imported by production code, creating a hidden src→test dependency not captured in the call graph. Zone health metrics for zones containing mixed src/ and test/ files should be flagged as unreliable until the production files are relocated.
[call graph] 309 internal calls, 1 outgoing, 22 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: CLI Interface Layer
zones.description: Implements the interactive and programmatic CLI surface of Rex including chunked review workflows, MCP server bootstrapping, output formatting, project configuration access, and miscellaneous management commands.
insights: 10 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports

The bidirectional flow between this zone and unit-analyze (56 inbound, 32 outbound) confirms the two zones have grown entangled; commands like analyze.ts and smart-add.ts that live in unit-analyze likely belong here conceptually.
src/cli/mcp.ts as an entry point is architecturally significant — the MCP server is the primary integration surface for Claude Code tooling and should be treated as a stable public API boundary.

chunked-review.ts and chunked-review-state.ts represent a stateful multi-turn interaction pattern that is distinct from other stateless commands; isolating this into its own sub-module would clarify the state management surface.

The bidirectional dependency with unit-analyze (cli → unit-analyze: 56, unit-analyze → cli: 32) and with core (cli → core: 38, core → cli: 15) suggests the CLI layer boundary is not enforced — business logic has leaked both in and out.
store/project-config.ts appearing as an entry point in this zone rather than in the store zone indicates a cross-zone boundary violation; this file should live in and be exported from the store zone.
cli/mcp.ts as an isolated entry point for MCP server bootstrapping is a clean separation of transport concerns from command logic.
Zone "cli-interface-layer" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The three-zone triangle of cli-interface-layer ↔ prd-analysis-engine ↔ prd-domain-operations (core), with all three pairs bidirectionally coupled and call volumes exceeding 50 in every direction, forms a dependency knot: no single zone can be extracted, mocked, or replaced without modifying the other two simultaneously.

The 65 call-graph edges from cli-interface-layer → prd-domain-operations combined with 15 reverse imports from core → cli means CLI command handlers are not just consuming domain operations — they are being called back by domain logic, suggesting domain state transitions are dispatching through CLI output utilities to report progress.

The three-way bidirectional coupling between cli-interface-layer, prd-analysis-engine, and prd-domain-operations (core) constitutes a dependency knot: all three zones import from each other with call volumes above 50 per pair. This prevents independent testing, extraction, or replacement of any single zone.

core → cli (15 imports, part of the 65 call-graph edges flowing back from domain into CLI) suggests domain operations are calling CLI output utilities for progress reporting. Side-effect I/O should be injected into domain logic, not imported from the CLI layer.

src/analyze/reshape-reason.ts is classified in cli-interface-layer despite residing in src/analyze/ — its name describes a domain transformation (reshaping analysis output), not a presentation concern; this is a misplaced file that contributes to the bidirectional coupling with prd-analysis-engine.

The CLI layer is the only mechanism for progress feedback from the analysis engine (214 inbound call-graph edges). This makes it structurally impossible to invoke the analysis engine from the MCP server, programmatic API, or any non-CLI context without importing CLI output utilities — a hidden runtime constraint with no indication at the type level.

src/analyze/reshape-reason.ts is misplaced: its path and purpose (transforming analysis data shapes) indicate it is domain logic belonging in prd-analysis-engine, not a CLI presentation concern. Its current placement is a primary source of the bidirectional cli ↔ prd-analysis-engine coupling.

The CLI output layer is the sole progress-reporting mechanism for the analysis engine (214 call-graph edges). Invoking prd-analysis-engine from the MCP HTTP server or any programmatic context silently imports and executes CLI output code. An EventEmitter or callback injection at the analyze/index.ts boundary would decouple the two zones entirely.

store → cli: 5 combined with cli → store: 20 means the persistence layer makes 5 callback imports into the CLI layer. A persistence store importing CLI utilities implies it contains CLI-aware error messages or output formatting — coupling storage semantics to terminal presentation. This is a secondary instance of the same domain→CLI inversion present in prd-analysis-engine, indicating the pattern is systemic rather than isolated.

src/cli/commands/constants.ts is imported by 6 zones and lives inside cli-interface-layer — every non-CLI zone that needs shared constants must take a transitive compile-time dependency on the CLI package. This means removing or renaming a CLI command forces a rebuild of every zone that uses a shared constant, even if the constant itself is unchanged. The blast radius of CLI changes is artificially amplified by this placement.

The chunked-review-state.ts file in cli-interface-layer introduces stateful session management into the CLI layer, making it the only stateful component in a layer that is otherwise a thin dispatch wrapper. This statefulness is architecturally anomalous for a CLI command module and will prevent the CLI layer from being safely parallelized or reused in non-interactive contexts (e.g., MCP batch operations).

store → cli: 5 imports indicate the persistence layer contains CLI-aware logic (likely output formatting or error messages). This is the same domain→CLI inversion found in prd-analysis-engine, confirming it is a package-wide pattern rather than an isolated case. A single shared output-primitives module extracted from cli-interface-layer would eliminate both inversion sites without restructuring either zone.

chunked-review-state.ts introduces stateful multi-turn session management directly into the CLI command layer. Session state held in CLI command modules cannot be accessed by the MCP server (src/cli/mcp.ts) or any programmatic caller — if chunked review is ever needed in non-interactive contexts, the state management layer must be duplicated. Extract session state into the store zone to make it transport-agnostic.

src/cli/commands/constants.ts has a 6-zone import fan-in but is classified inside cli-interface-layer. Its presence there forces every consumer zone to take a compile-time dependency on the CLI package boundary even when only needing a constant value. Moving it to a standalone shared-constants module would reduce cli-interface-layer's artificial import fan-out and decouple constants from CLI volatility.
[call graph] 1382 internal calls, 207 outgoing, 305 incoming (cohesion: 0.87, coupling: 0.13)

zones.name: Fix Command Module
zones.description: Encapsulates the PRD fix command, pairing the CLI entry point with its core logic and unit tests in a tight four-file cluster.
insights: High coupling (0.6) — 3 imports target "prd-analysis-engine"
The co-location of CLI command, core implementation, and both test files in one zone reflects a well-scoped vertical slice pattern.
Cohesion at 0.4 is borderline; if fix.ts core logic grows, consider whether it should absorb into the broader core zone or remain isolated.
The zone's small size makes it a good candidate for merging with the CLI Interface Layer zone if the fix command doesn't warrant its own module boundary.
Cohesion of 0.4 and coupling of 0.6 sit at the warning threshold; monitor as the fix command evolves to ensure it doesn't become a hidden dependency hub.
With 136 internal calls and only 1 incoming, this zone is a true terminal leaf — no other zone depends on it, making it the safest zone to refactor or relocate without risk of downstream breakage.
fix-command-module is a terminal leaf zone (1 incoming edge) — safe to refactor or merge without downstream impact.

136 internal calls across 4 files yields a per-file internal call density of 34 — the highest in the batch — indicating deeply self-referential logic that is never reused externally (1 incoming edge). If that single incoming edge is the CLI router, the entire zone's complexity is programmatically inaccessible as a library.

The import-cohesion (0.4) and call-graph cohesion (0.9) gap of 0.5 is the second-widest in the batch; the zone is functionally cohesive at runtime but structurally fragmented at the import level, meaning the low cohesion score reflects import style rather than logical incoherence.

fix-command-module has 136 internal calls but only 1 incoming external call. If the single incoming edge is the CLI entry-point router, the entire zone's implementation complexity is dead from a library-reuse perspective — any programmatic consumer would have to go through the CLI boundary, making testing and reuse unnecessarily difficult.

Zone ID uses the suffix '-module' — the only zone in the package with a structural suffix rather than a domain/responsibility suffix. All other zones use domain terms (prd-domain-operations, persistence-sync-layer, token-usage-analytics). 'fix-command' would be consistent with the implicit naming convention.

136 internal calls across 4 files with a single incoming edge from the CLI router is a 'gold-plating' signature: implementation complexity was built in isolation and then minimally integrated. Over-engineered isolated modules accumulate test debt without proportional reuse value.

Rename zone from 'fix-command-module' to 'fix-command' to align with the domain/responsibility naming convention used by all other zones; the '-module' suffix is a structural term that adds no semantic information and breaks the implicit taxonomy.

136 internal calls in a 4-file zone with 1 incoming edge is a gold-plating risk: if the sole incoming edge is the CLI router, the entire implementation surface is unreachable as a library. Add at least one direct programmatic test of the core fix logic bypassing the CLI boundary before the zone grows further.
[call graph] 136 internal calls, 15 outgoing, 1 incoming (cohesion: 0.9, coupling: 0.1)

zones.name: Monorepo Root
zones.description: Top-level project scaffolding including documentation, branding assets, shared TypeScript and Vitest configuration, and the root package manifest.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
The root vitest.config.ts likely acts as the shared test runner configuration referenced by all child packages; verify package-level configs extend rather than duplicate it.
context.md and llms.txt at the root are AI-consumption artifacts produced by sourcevision; treat them as build outputs and ensure they are regenerated as part of the CI pipeline.
Having zero coupling from this zone confirms the root config layer is not accidentally imported by any package, which is the correct pattern.
Root-level context.md and llms.txt should be .gitignore-listed or explicitly marked as generated artifacts to prevent stale AI context from being committed.
Perfect cohesion and zero coupling confirm the root zone is correctly isolated — no cross-package import leaks at the top level.

zones.name: Persistence & Sync Layer
zones.description: Manages all PRD persistence through a pluggable adapter registry, file-based storage, and integration schemas for external systems like Jira and Notion.
insights: 9 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
Cohesion of 0.6 is the strongest in this batch, indicating the store zone has clear, well-bounded responsibilities around persistence and adapters.
The presence of integration-schemas (jira, notion) alongside the core file adapter suggests a clean extension pattern — new integrations can be added without touching core store contracts.
The sync CLI command living inside the store zone is appropriate given the tight coupling between sync logic and adapter contracts; this keeps the feature vertical.
Good cohesion (0.6) and moderate coupling (0.4) make this the most architecturally sound zone in the batch — the adapter registry pattern is a strong extensibility foundation.
Zone "persistence-sync-layer" has files across 8 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The persistence-sync-layer is the only zone in the batch with no participation in any detected circular dependency chain, confirming the adapter registry pattern as an effective isolation mechanism worth replicating elsewhere.
persistence-sync-layer is the only zone not involved in any circular dependency chain — its adapter registry boundary pattern should be used as the template when decomposing over-coupled zones.

persistence-sync-layer has nearly balanced incoming/outgoing call-graph traffic (53 in, 48 out — ratio 1.1:1), the most symmetric of any zone in the batch. Despite being the healthiest zone by import metrics, the call-graph symmetry reveals mutual call dependency with its consumers, meaning persistence changes are as likely to break callers as caller changes are to break persistence — a bilateral risk not visible from import counts alone.

persistence-sync-layer spans 8 directories yet achieves cohesion 0.6 — the highest in the batch. This directly contradicts the common heuristic that directory spread correlates with low cohesion. The adapter registry pattern is the causal mechanism: the registry acts as a gravitational center that keeps all spread files logically unified regardless of physical location.

The zone's 9 entry points across 8 directories with no zone-to-zone circular dependency participation is the strongest empirical argument in the package for the adapter registry pattern as a coupling-containment mechanism. This evidence should be documented as a design decision record (ADR) to justify replicating the pattern when decomposing the bidirectionally-coupled zones.

Document the adapter registry pattern as an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) capturing why it achieves zero circular dependency participation despite spanning 8 directories. This prevents future contributors from 'simplifying' the registry away without understanding its architectural role as the package's only proven coupling-containment mechanism.
[call graph] 231 internal calls, 48 outgoing, 53 incoming (cohesion: 0.83, coupling: 0.17)

zones.name: PRD Analysis Engine
zones.description: The central Rex analysis subsystem encompassing PRD schema definitions, decomposition, deduplication, validation, guided analysis, and the majority of CLI command implementations.
insights: 24 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
At 71 files this is the largest zone in the package by a significant margin; it is a strong candidate for splitting into at least two sub-zones: schema/validation and analysis-pipeline.

The bidirectional import cycle with the cli zone (unit-analyze → cli: 32, cli → unit-analyze: 56) is an architectural smell — CLI commands should depend on the analysis engine, not the reverse; the reverse imports likely indicate shared utilities that should be extracted into a neutral layer.
The 24 entry points indicate this zone is the most referenced hub in the entire package; any instability here has wide blast radius.

Bidirectional coupling with both the cli zone and the core zone creates implicit circular dependency risk; the unit-analyze → cli imports (32 edges) should be audited and moved to a shared utility zone.
With 71 files and 24 entry points, this zone lacks a single clear public surface; introducing a dedicated facade module would reduce the coupling surface and improve navigability.
The presence of both schema/v1.ts and schema/index.ts as entry points is good hygiene — the barrel export pattern keeps external consumers decoupled from internal schema evolution.
Zone "prd-analysis-engine" has files across 10 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The call graph exposes an architectural inversion: prd-analysis-engine makes 214 calls INTO cli-interface-layer vs receiving only 107 back. The domain engine is more dependent on the presentation layer than the presentation layer is on the domain — the dependency arrow is pointing in the wrong direction.

With 201 incoming call-graph edges and 318 outgoing, prd-analysis-engine has a net outbound bias — it is actively reaching into every other zone rather than being a stable dependency target. This is the opposite of what a domain hub should look like.

The 41 call-graph edges to token-usage-analytics indicate observability instrumentation is inlined throughout the analysis engine rather than injected at the boundary — coupling domain logic directly to analytics collection.

Call graph shows prd-analysis-engine → cli-interface-layer at 214 calls versus cli-interface-layer → prd-analysis-engine at 107. The domain analysis engine is twice as dependent on the CLI layer as the reverse — a clear dependency inversion that must be resolved before either zone can be safely refactored.

prd-analysis-engine makes 41 call-graph calls to token-usage-analytics, indicating analytics instrumentation is scattered throughout domain logic rather than applied at a boundary via injection or a decorator — couples the domain engine to observability infrastructure.

The 24-entry-point to 71-file ratio (33.8%) means encapsulation is effectively absent — this zone behaves as a flat namespace; a module with meaningful boundaries should expose fewer than 10% of its files as entry points.

Making prd-analysis-engine simultaneously a dependency of cli-interface-layer AND a dependent of it means there is no valid topological sort for this pair — the zone cannot be compiled, tested, or replaced in isolation under any layering scheme.

The 41 call-graph edges to token-usage-analytics are inlined throughout domain logic rather than at a boundary, making it impossible to unit-test the analysis engine without a real analytics sink — analytics coupling directly prevents hermetic testing.

33.8% entry-point ratio (24 of 71 files exposed) means the zone has no encapsulation boundary — it functions as a flat namespace. A domain engine should expose at most 3–5 operations via a single facade module; the current surface makes every internal file a de-facto public contract.

Analytics instrumentation (41 call-graph edges to token-usage-analytics) is scattered throughout domain logic with no injection point, making the analysis engine untestable without a live analytics sink. A single analytics-collection boundary at the analyze/index.ts entrypoint would eliminate all 41 internal edges.

Cross-zone import table shows unit-analyze → unit: 12, meaning prd-analysis-engine (production source code) imports 12 times from the 'unit' zone — a 4-file zone whose name and low cohesion (0.29) indicate it is test infrastructure. Production code importing from test utilities is an architectural inversion: it makes test helpers load-bearing at runtime and prevents the test zone from being excluded from production builds.

core → unit-analyze: 67 is the single largest cross-zone import count in the entire graph, meaning prd-domain-operations is the package's most intensive consumer of prd-analysis-engine internals — domain operations orchestrate the analysis engine more than any other caller, including the CLI. This inverts the expected dependency direction: if analysis feeds domain state, domain operations should receive analysis results, not reach into analysis internals 67 times.

The 24 entry points in prd-analysis-engine combined with zero direct import path from recommendation-pipeline to cli means recommendation results are routed through prd-analysis-engine before reaching the CLI layer — the analysis engine is acting as an unintentional message bus between the recommendation pipeline and CLI output, coupling three zones through an implicit intermediary.

prd-analysis-engine imports 12 times from the 'unit' test zone (unit-analyze → unit: 12 in cross-zone table). Production modules must not import test infrastructure — audit and extract any shared fixtures or helpers into a dedicated non-test utility module. This is a runtime correctness risk if test-only modules have side effects or conditional logic.

The zone is named 'prd-analysis-engine' but its files live under src/analyze/ — the directory name and zone name are inconsistent. All consumers and contributors must translate between the two names, increasing cognitive load. Either rename the zone to match the directory ('analyze') or move the files to src/prd-analysis/ to match the zone name.
[call graph] 2839 internal calls, 318 outgoing, 201 incoming (cohesion: 0.9, coupling: 0.1)

zones.name: PRD Domain Operations
zones.description: The primary domain layer containing all PRD tree manipulation, lifecycle, health, analytics, and reorganization logic that underpins the rest of the system.
insights: 23 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
At 47 files, this zone is large but maintains acceptable cohesion (0.51); its 23 entry points signal it is a genuine shared library rather than a monolithic blob.
The zone receives heavy inbound coupling from unit-analyze (22 imports) and cli (38 imports), confirming its role as the foundational domain API — this is expected and healthy.
Consider grouping the many removal operations (remove-epic, remove-feature, remove-task, delete) under a sub-namespace to clarify intent and reduce cognitive load for new contributors.
23 entry points in a 47-file zone is high; publishing a narrower public surface via public.ts and deprecating direct sub-path imports would reduce accidental coupling.

Core and cli have bidirectional imports (core → cli: 15, cli → core: 38), creating a soft circular dependency risk at the zone boundary that should be resolved by ensuring cli never re-exports into core.

The 50 outgoing calls to prd-analysis-engine from prd-domain-operations suggest domain operations depend on analysis results at runtime — this inversion (domain depending on analysis) should be reviewed; domain logic should typically be analysis-agnostic.

prd-domain-operations → prd-analysis-engine has 50 call-graph edges; domain-layer code depending on analysis-layer output is an inversion of the expected dependency direction and risks coupling stable domain logic to volatile analysis heuristics.

prd-domain-operations has the highest internal call volume (1591) of any zone in the batch, and outgoing calls represent only 4.6% of total calls (77 of 1668 total). It is structurally the closest zone to a stable dependency foundation — the sole obstacle is the 50-call outgoing dependency on prd-analysis-engine. Severing that one dependency corridor would promote this zone to a true stable sink.

The import-cohesion (0.51) vs call-graph cohesion (0.95) gap of 0.44 indicates the zone is functionally tight at runtime; its many entry points inflate the import-based coupling score without reflecting actual call-time fragmentation.

The 50 outgoing call-graph edges from prd-domain-operations to prd-analysis-engine mean that stable domain logic depends on volatile analysis heuristics at the function-call level, not just the import level. A format change in any analysis output type propagates directly into domain operation logic, inverting the stability hierarchy. Introducing a typed result interface owned by domain operations and implemented by analysis would break this dependency direction.

src/core/analytics.ts is classified in prd-domain-operations while src/core/token-usage.ts is classified in token-usage-analytics. Both files live under src/core/ and are analytically related, creating a directory-zone mismatch: a developer browsing src/core/ encounters files belonging to two different zones with no visual boundary between them. This violates the principle of least surprise for directory navigation.

At 47 files and 23 entry points, prd-domain-operations has no observable public.ts gate — the CLAUDE.md contract (src/public.ts as the sole public surface) appears unenforced here. Without a public surface gate, the 23 entry points grow organically and the 128 incoming call-graph edges attach to arbitrary internal files rather than a declared API.

src/core/analytics.ts belongs to a different zone than src/core/token-usage.ts despite sharing the same directory and conceptual domain. Either relocate analytics.ts into token-usage-analytics or explicitly document in CLAUDE.md that analytics.ts is a general-purpose utility distinct from token-usage calculations to prevent future misclassification.

23 entry points with 128 incoming call-graph edges and no visible public.ts gate means callers attach to arbitrary internal files. Enforce the src/public.ts contract by adding a CI lint rule that rejects cross-zone imports not routed through public.ts — this converts 128 uncontrolled attachment points into a single versioned surface.
[call graph] 1591 internal calls, 77 outgoing, 128 incoming (cohesion: 0.95, coupling: 0.05)

zones.name: Recommendation Pipeline
zones.description: Handles the full lifecycle of LLM-generated proposals: extracting structured suggestions from AI output, acknowledging them, reconciling with existing PRD state, and converting accepted recommendations into PRD items.
insights: High coupling (0.52) — 22 imports target "prd-analysis-engine"

The moderate coupling (0.52) is driven by this zone's 22 outbound imports into unit-analyze — recommendations are tightly coupled to the PRD schema and analysis types, which is architecturally appropriate.
The recommend/types.ts file should be the single contract point between this pipeline and consumers; verify it is the only file imported by external zones rather than internal service files.
conflict-detection.ts and create-from-recommendations.ts are the core business logic files; ensure they have direct unit tests since errors here silently corrupt the PRD tree.

The cohesion of 0.48 is borderline — the mix of CLI command (recommend.ts), service logic, and type definitions suggests this zone spans two concerns: proposal ingestion and recommendation-to-PRD conversion.
scanners.ts and extract.ts are listed as entry points, indicating multiple callers reach into the internals of this pipeline rather than going through a single facade.
Zone "recommendation-pipeline" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The 22:12 import ratio (outbound to prd-analysis-engine vs inbound from it) makes this the most directionally clean major dependency pair in the graph — it is the closest approximation to a proper layered interface in the codebase.

The 4 incoming call-graph edges suggest recommendation-pipeline is consumed by very few callers despite being a distinct pipeline — if only cli commands trigger it, the pipeline boundary is effectively well-guarded even without a formal facade.

recommendation-pipeline → prd-analysis-engine is the healthiest cross-zone dependency in the package: 22 outbound, 12 inbound, nearly unidirectional. Treat this pair as the reference model for how other zones should relate to prd-analysis-engine.

At cohesion 0.48 the recommendation-pipeline zone sits below the minimum viable isolation threshold — it provides no encapsulation benefit over simply placing its files in prd-analysis-engine. The moderate cohesion is a signal that the community detector found weak internal bonding, not that the zone represents a genuine architectural boundary.

reconcile.ts by name is the merge/conflict-resolution step between incoming proposals and existing PRD state; if it imports from both recommendation-pipeline and prd-analysis-engine, it is the natural extraction point for the shared interface that would break the bidirectional coupling between the two zones.

cohesion of 0.48 is below the viable zone threshold. At this level the zone boundary adds conceptual overhead without providing isolation. The pipeline should either be absorbed into prd-analysis-engine with a clear subdirectory boundary, or its types.ts promoted as a strict one-way contract and its cohesion raised above 0.6 by removing misclassified files.

recommendation-pipeline is the only zone that simultaneously has the lowest import-cohesion (0.48) AND the highest coupling (0.52) among all non-data zones — this exact profile (high coupling + low cohesion) is the textbook definition of a fragile zone: changes from any direction propagate through a module that provides no isolation in return.

src/analyze/acknowledge.ts and src/analyze/scanners.ts carry names describing analysis operations (scanning, acknowledgment) rather than recommendation operations — their classification into recommendation-pipeline by import-proximity suggests they were pulled across the zone boundary by shared type imports, not by functional role. Their names are semantic mismatches with their assigned zone.

There is no direct import path from recommendation-pipeline to cli-interface-layer in the cross-zone table — all recommendation output reaches the CLI only after passing through prd-analysis-engine. This hidden mediation means recommendation result formatting is embedded in the analysis engine rather than being a first-class CLI concern, making it invisible to CLI-layer refactors.

recommendation-pipeline has the worst cohesion/coupling ratio in the package (0.48/0.52) — both metrics simultaneously at their worst. This zone provides no isolation benefit while adding routing overhead for every change. Flag as fragile and prioritize absorption into prd-analysis-engine with a clear subdirectory boundary before any adjacent refactor touches shared types.

src/analyze/acknowledge.ts and src/analyze/scanners.ts have names describing analysis concerns but are classified in recommendation-pipeline — rename or relocate to eliminate the semantic mismatch that misleads zone boundary interpretation.
[call graph] 663 internal calls, 72 outgoing, 4 incoming (cohesion: 0.9, coupling: 0.1)

zones.name: Rex Project State
zones.description: Runtime data directory holding the live PRD tree, execution log, project config, and workflow state for the Rex tool.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

These four files are pure data artifacts, not source modules — the perfect cohesion and zero coupling reflect that the community detector found no import edges, which is expected for JSON/JSONL/Markdown files.
Changes to prd.json schema should be coordinated with the schema types in the rex source package to avoid silent deserialization failures.
The execution-log.jsonl append-only pattern is a good audit trail; ensure tooling never rewrites it wholesale on a re-run.
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling are expected for a data-only directory — no action needed.
prd.json is the single source of truth for all task state; any direct manual edits bypass validation logic in the analyze pipeline.

zones.name: Token Usage Analytics
zones.description: Tracks and reports LLM token consumption, pairing the usage CLI command with its core calculation logic and regression tests.
insights: Low cohesion (0.29) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
High coupling (0.71) — 3 imports target "cli-interface-layer"
Low cohesion (0.29) and high coupling (0.71) suggest token-usage logic may have too many external dependencies for its small size — worth auditing what core and store modules it pulls in.
The presence of a dedicated regression test file indicates this feature has had correctness issues historically; keep this test file and expand it as usage calculation logic evolves.
If token-usage.ts is consumed broadly, it should be promoted into the core zone's public surface rather than sitting in an isolated low-cohesion cluster.

Cohesion of 0.29 with coupling of 0.71 is the weakest profile in the batch; the token-usage module likely imports heavily from core utilities and may be better absorbed into the PRD Domain Operations zone.

42 incoming calls into a 4-file zone means each file in token-usage-analytics receives ~10.5 inbound calls on average — the highest fan-in density in the batch. This hidden utility role is not reflected in the zone boundary and should be made explicit.

token-usage-analytics has the highest inbound call density (42 incoming / 4 files = ~10.5 per file), indicating it acts as a hidden shared utility; promote it to an explicit public API surface or absorb it into the core zone with a stable export contract.

The import-cohesion (0.29) to call-graph cohesion (0.92) gap of 0.63 is the widest in the entire batch, indicating that token-usage-analytics imports many modules for type definitions and narrow utilities without making deep call chains into them. The Louvain import-proximity score severely overstates the coupling problem; the zone is functionally self-contained and the low cohesion score is a measurement artifact of its wide import surface, not a reflection of logical fragmentation.

42 incoming call-graph edges into a 4-file zone (10.5 per file) combined with import-cohesion of 0.29 creates a dangerous asymmetry: callers are tightly bound to this zone's implementation while the zone itself appears loosely structured. Any interface change in token-usage.ts propagates to all 42 call sites with no stable contract acting as a buffer — promoting it to an explicit public API with a versioned interface would protect all 42 callers from internal refactoring churn.

token-usage-analytics and fix-command-module are both 4-file micro-zones with cohesion ≤ 0.4. Zones of ≤ 4 files are structurally incapable of achieving high import-cohesion because a small cluster lacks sufficient internal edges to compensate for any external import diversity. The low cohesion scores for both micro-zones are partially a measurement artifact of zone size, not evidence of logical fragmentation. A minimum zone size threshold (e.g. 8 files) in the Louvain configuration would prevent spurious micro-zone creation.

The regression test file (token-usage-regression.test.ts) signals historical correctness failures. Combined with 42 incoming call-graph edges and no stable public contract, any future fix to token calculation logic risks silently breaking all 42 callers. The regression test exists precisely to catch this, but its scope is limited to what was previously broken — not to future interface changes. A formal interface contract is the missing complement to the regression suite.

Configure a minimum zone size floor of 8 files in the Louvain parameters to prevent micro-zone creation. Both 4-file zones (token-usage-analytics cohesion 0.29, fix-command-module cohesion 0.4) fall below the measurement reliability threshold for import-cohesion, generating misleading low-cohesion alerts for zones that are functionally self-contained (call-graph cohesion 0.92 and 0.9 respectively).

token-usage-analytics has a regression test but no interface contract: 42 incoming callers are protected against previously-known bugs but not against future interface changes. Define an explicit TypeScript interface for token-usage inputs and outputs in a shared types file so that any breaking change becomes a compile error across all 42 callers rather than a runtime regression.

3 circular dependency chains detected — see imports.json for details

The most significant architectural issue is the pervasive bidirectional coupling across unit-analyze, cli, and core — all three zones import from each other, which undermines the intended layered hierarchy and will make refactoring any single zone difficult.

The store zone (not in this batch, cohesion: 0.6) also has bidirectional ties with both cli and core; a dedicated shared-utilities or infrastructure zone extracting the commonly imported primitives would break these cycles cleanly.
The .rex and root zones are correctly isolated data/config layers with zero coupling, demonstrating good separation between runtime artifacts and source code.

The bidirectional import relationship between core and cli zones (core → cli: 15, cli → core: 38) is the most significant architectural risk in the graph; domain logic in core should never need to import from CLI command handlers — any shared utilities flowing upward from cli into core should be extracted into a neutral utility module.

The unit-analyze zone (PRD Analysis Engine) acts as a cross-cutting hub with heavy inbound and outbound coupling to core, cli, unit, and store simultaneously; this many-to-many coupling pattern suggests it may be doing too much and could benefit from decomposition into a pure analysis phase and a separate proposal/recommendation phase.

The store zone achieves the best cohesion/coupling balance (0.6/0.4) in the entire graph, validating the adapter registry pattern as an effective boundary mechanism — other zones should aspire to this profile.

The token-usage and fix-command zones are micro-clusters with low cohesion that exist at zone boundaries; both are candidates for absorption into their nearest high-cohesion neighbor (core or cli) to reduce fragmentation without losing test coverage.

Cross-zone import counts reveal that unit-analyze is the most coupled zone overall (receives 67 from core, 56 from cli, 22 from unit, 20 from store); if this zone is the PRD analysis engine, those import volumes suggest it is the system's integration point and should be treated as a stable public API with versioned contracts.

Three of five analyzed zones have cohesion below 0.52, suggesting the community detection algorithm is grouping files by import proximity rather than by domain role; manual zone boundary refinement (or explicit zone config) would produce more meaningful groupings.
The unit-analyze zone at 71 files dwarfs all others (next largest is cli at 38); this imbalance typically indicates one zone is doing the work of two or three and should be a refactoring priority.
Cross-zone imports total over 300 edges among just five zones, with the highest-traffic corridors all involving unit-analyze; this zone is the de-facto coupling hub of the rex package.

Five zones have cohesion below 0.4 (unit-core: 0.29, e2e: 0.29) or sit at the warning threshold (unit-cli: 0.4); consolidating the two micro-zones (unit-cli, unit-core) into their parent domains would raise average cohesion across the package.

The overall zone graph has no isolated sinks — every zone participates in cross-zone imports — indicating a well-connected but potentially over-coupled package where changes in core will ripple broadly.
Integration schemas (jira, notion) being co-located with the file adapter in the persistence zone is a clean design choice that avoids polluting domain logic with third-party shape concerns.
src/analyze/ contains files from 3 zones (cli-interface-layer, prd-analysis-engine, recommendation-pipeline) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

The data-only zones (rex-project-state, monorepo-root) achieve perfect isolation (cohesion 1, coupling 0) and serve as proof that the package CAN produce clean zone boundaries — the pervasive bidirectional coupling in the source zones is a design choice, not a tooling limitation.

The call graph ratios reveal a consistent pattern: every zone makes more calls INTO other zones than it receives, except for the data zones. This means no zone acts as a true stable dependency target — the codebase is organized as a peer mesh, not a layered hierarchy.

The unit zone (4 files, 22 imports from unit → unit-analyze) indicates test utility helpers are tightly bound to prd-analysis-engine internals. If prd-analysis-engine is ever split, these test helpers will need to be redistributed or refactored simultaneously — raising the cost of decomposition.

The three circular dependency chains (previously noted) are structurally concentrated in the cli ↔ core ↔ unit-analyze triangle. Resolving even one bidirectional pair in this triangle (e.g., eliminating core → cli) would break at least one circular chain without touching the other zones.

The call graph asymmetry between prd-analysis-engine and cli-interface-layer (214 calls engine→cli vs 107 cli→engine) reveals that the analysis engine contains embedded CLI presentation concerns rather than returning pure data — the higher-volume direction should be inverted by extracting a result-formatting layer that cli owns exclusively.

Three circular dependency chains exist in a package where the call graph shows over 300 cross-zone edges concentrated through one hub (unit-analyze/prd-analysis-engine); each circular chain runs through this hub, meaning resolving even one chain requires touching the most-coupled zone in the package.

The two lowest-cohesion zones (token-usage-analytics: 0.29, cli-e2e-test-suite: 0.29) are both artifacts of import-proximity clustering rather than domain coherence — neither represents a genuine architectural boundary, and both should be dissolved rather than maintained as explicit zones.

No zone in this package is a pure sink (zero outgoing cross-zone imports) except the test zones; the absence of any stable, read-only domain object layer means every zone is simultaneously a consumer and a provider, which amplifies the blast radius of any interface change.

The bidirectional core↔cli coupling (core→cli: 15, cli→core: 38) combined with the 65 cli→prd-domain-operations call-graph edges suggests the CLI layer has grown into a secondary orchestration layer; extracting an explicit use-case or application-service layer between cli and core would eliminate both the bidirectional coupling and the need for core to import cli utilities.

No zone in the source layer acts as a true stable dependency sink: every zone has higher outbound call volume than inbound. A layered architecture requires at least one foundation zone that only receives calls; the absence of such a zone means the entire graph is a peer mesh with no enforced dependency direction.

The data-only zones (rex-project-state, monorepo-root) achieve perfect cohesion and zero coupling, confirming the tooling can identify clean boundaries. The bidirectional coupling in source zones reflects architectural choices that can be reversed — this is a refactoring problem, not a measurement artifact.

prd-analysis-engine acts as a coupling hub with bidirectional ties to every other major zone (cli, core, store, unit, token-usage); any interface change in this zone propagates to the entire package — it requires a versioned public contract and strict change-review discipline.

All three detected circular dependency chains pass through the prd-analysis-engine zone; breaking any cycle requires restructuring this zone's imports, making it the single highest-priority refactoring target in the package.

The fix-command-module and token-usage-analytics micro-zones (4 files each) contribute to zone fragmentation without providing meaningful isolation — consolidating them into their dominant neighbor zones would reduce total zone count, raise average cohesion, and simplify the dependency graph without losing any functionality.

Test infrastructure (unit zone, 4 files) imports 22 times from prd-analysis-engine internals, coupling test helpers to the largest and most unstable zone. Any decomposition of prd-analysis-engine will require coordinated updates to test utilities, raising the effective cost of splitting that zone.

The call graph shows 214 edges from prd-analysis-engine into cli-interface-layer — the analysis engine is calling CLI output/formatting functions directly, embedding presentation logic inside domain analysis code; this is a leaky abstraction that should be resolved by moving all output formatting into cli and having analysis return plain data structures.

src/cli/commands/constants.ts and src/cli/output.ts are each imported by 6 zones — more than any domain module. Files with this import fan-in are de-facto global infrastructure and should live in a dedicated shared-infrastructure zone, not inside the CLI commands directory. Their current location forces every zone to take a compile-time dependency on the CLI layer even when it needs only shared constants or output formatting utilities.

No zone in the source layer exports a pure DTO or value-object layer. All cross-zone communication passes rich domain objects directly, meaning any internal field rename or type change in a domain object propagates immediately across every zone boundary with no API-contract buffer. Introducing a thin DTO/transfer-types module as the sole cross-zone currency would convert all 300+ cross-zone import edges into dependencies on a single stable schema file.

The three circular dependency chains are all structurally contingent on prd-analysis-engine importing from cli-interface-layer. Eliminating that single directional edge (214 call-graph calls, achievable by injecting a progress-callback interface) would break all three chains simultaneously without touching any other zone — making it the highest-leverage single refactoring available in the package.

The MCP server (src/cli/mcp.ts) is listed as an entry point in cli-interface-layer, meaning the primary programmatic integration surface for Claude Code tooling is co-located with interactive terminal commands. The MCP server has fundamentally different stability and versioning requirements than CLI commands and should be extracted into its own zone or at minimum its own directory to isolate it from CLI churn.

The call-graph shows no zone with higher inbound than outbound call volume in the source layer. A healthy layered architecture requires at least one foundation zone that is a pure dependency sink (zero outbound cross-zone calls). The absence of any such zone confirms the entire source graph is a peer mesh — there is no enforced dependency direction and no zone can be safely replaced without understanding the full graph.

A systematic divergence between import-cohesion and call-graph cohesion exists across all five zones (average gap ≈ 0.5). Zones with the lowest import-cohesion scores (token-usage: 0.29→0.92, e2e: 0.29→1.0) are actually the most functionally self-contained at runtime. Zone health decisions based solely on Louvain import-cohesion will consistently misidentify well-scoped functional modules as fragmentation candidates. Call-graph cohesion should be the primary signal for zone boundary quality.

The package has exactly one zone approaching stable-sink status (prd-domain-operations: 95% internal call ratio) but is prevented from achieving it by a single 50-edge dependency on prd-analysis-engine. All other zones have outgoing call ratios above 6%, confirming the peer-mesh topology. Resolving the prd-domain-operations → prd-analysis-engine dependency would create the package's first true foundation layer and provide a stable base for decomposing the bidirectionally-coupled zones above it.

The persistence-sync-layer's near-1:1 incoming/outgoing call ratio (53:48) is architecturally distinct from the other zones and indicates it functions as a mediator or bridge rather than a provider or consumer. This bilateral call pattern means it cannot be safely refactored without coordinating changes on both sides simultaneously — its mediator role should be made explicit in documentation and its interface contracts formalized before any adjacent zone is modified.

No zone in the batch has both high import-cohesion AND high call-graph cohesion simultaneously. The two metrics are inversely correlated across the five zones analyzed, suggesting the current zone boundaries were drawn to minimize import-graph edge cuts (Louvain objective) rather than to maximize functional cohesion. A boundary redraw using call-graph community detection rather than import-graph community detection would likely produce structurally different and more stable zone assignments.

src/cli/commands/constants.ts and src/cli/output.ts function as global shared infrastructure but are classified inside the CLI commands directory. Their 6-zone import fan-in forces every domain zone to take a transitive dependency on the CLI package. Extracting these files into a dedicated shared-types or shared-infrastructure zone would reduce the import fan-in of cli-interface-layer and eliminate several of the bidirectional coupling pairs.

No DTO or value-object boundary exists between zones: all cross-zone communication uses rich internal domain objects directly. Any field-level change to a domain type immediately becomes a breaking change across all 300+ cross-zone import edges simultaneously. A single transfer-types module as the sole cross-zone currency would scope the blast radius of type changes to one file.

The MCP server entry point (src/cli/mcp.ts) is co-located with interactive CLI commands in cli-interface-layer. MCP is a stable programmatic API consumed by external tooling; CLI commands are volatile interactive utilities. Co-location means MCP stability is coupled to CLI churn — a CLI refactor can silently break MCP consumers. The MCP transport layer should be an independent zone with its own versioning discipline.

Import-cohesion and call-graph cohesion are inversely correlated across all five analyzed zones, indicating zone boundaries were optimized for import-graph topology rather than runtime call behavior. Using import-proximity alone as the zone boundary criterion will consistently split functionally cohesive modules (high call-graph cohesion) while grouping functionally unrelated files that share imports. A hybrid boundary criterion weighting call-graph community membership more heavily would produce more actionable and stable zone definitions.

The package has no zone with both zero circular dependency participation AND significant incoming call volume except persistence-sync-layer, which achieves this at the cost of symmetric bilateral coupling. The absence of any unambiguously downstream-only foundation zone means every refactoring in the package carries bilateral blast radius — there is no safe base layer to refactor from the bottom up. Establishing prd-domain-operations as a true sink (by removing its prd-analysis-engine dependency) is a prerequisite for any systematic decomposition of the bidirectionally-coupled zones.

The cross-zone import table shows unit-analyze → unit: 12, where 'unit' is a 4-file test zone with cohesion 0.29. This means production code in the package's largest zone imports from test infrastructure 12 times — a systemic inversion that makes test helpers load-bearing at runtime and cannot be resolved by test-only build exclusions alone.

store → unit-analyze: 20 combined with store → cli: 5 means the persistence layer imports from both the analysis engine AND the CLI layer. A persistence store should be a pure I/O boundary with zero domain or presentation dependencies; these 25 cross-zone imports indicate the store zone has absorbed application-layer orchestration logic that belongs in a use-case or service layer above it.

The cross-zone import asymmetry between core → unit-analyze (67) and unit-analyze → core (22) reveals that prd-domain-operations is 3x more dependent on prd-analysis-engine than the reverse. Domain operations should consume analysis results, not reach into analysis internals; the 67-import dependency suggests domain operations are directly manipulating analysis intermediate state rather than consuming a published result type.

Three zones (recommendation-pipeline, prd-analysis-engine, cli-interface-layer) all have cohesion and coupling values within ±0.04 of 0.50, clustering near the 50/50 split. This statistical clustering is not coincidental — it indicates the Louvain community detector found no strong natural boundary between these three zones and placed them at the midpoint of the partition quality function. The three zones may form a single functional module that the detector split arbitrarily; treating them as three distinct architectural boundaries may be misleading.

The package has no cross-zone import edge flowing from test zones (unit, e2e) INTO source zones EXCEPT for unit-analyze → unit: 12, which flows in the wrong direction. All other test zones consume production code, as expected. The single reversed edge in prd-analysis-engine is an isolated violation but in the largest and most-coupled zone — making it the highest-risk instance of a test/production boundary inversion in the package.

The implicit convention that CLI output formatting is the sole progress-reporting mechanism for domain logic (214 call-graph edges prd-analysis-engine→cli) is undocumented and unenforceable. Any new integration surface (MCP, programmatic API, CI pipeline) that invokes the analysis engine will silently import and execute CLI terminal output code unless a developer already knows this convention exists. This implicit constraint should be captured as an explicit architectural decision record (ADR) until the coupling is resolved.

Zone IDs across the package use four distinct naming taxonomies simultaneously: feature-scope ('fix-command-module'), domain-responsibility ('prd-domain-operations'), infrastructure-layer ('persistence-sync-layer'), analytics ('token-usage-analytics'), and test-suite ('cli-e2e-test-suite'). No single naming convention is applied consistently. A uniform convention such as '{domain}-{responsibility}' (dropping structural suffixes like '-module', '-suite', '-layer') would make zone IDs self-describing and machine-comparable.

The two micro-zones (fix-command-module and token-usage-analytics, both 4 files) and the e2e zone (cli-e2e-test-suite, 16 files) are all below cohesion 0.4. All three are outliers produced by the Louvain algorithm grouping files that share test infrastructure or narrow CLI scope rather than domain boundaries. These three zones account for 24 files but produce disproportionate noise in zone health metrics. Excluding test-only zones and enforcing a minimum zone size would reduce the false-positive alert rate for the remaining source zones.

The CLAUDE.md documents src/public.ts as the canonical public API surface for all packages, but no zone in the rex package analysis references a public.ts file as a boundary enforcement mechanism. The absence of public.ts discipline in rex means the 23 entry points in prd-domain-operations and 9 in persistence-sync-layer grow by convention rather than by contract — any file can become a de-facto public API simply by being imported.

src/core/ contains files from at least two zones (prd-domain-operations and token-usage-analytics). src/analyze/ contains files from three zones (noted in previous insights). src/cli/commands/ spans multiple zones. No directory in the rex package corresponds 1:1 to a zone boundary. This systematic directory-zone mismatch means directory navigation and zone navigation are independent mental models that a developer must maintain simultaneously — a strong argument for introducing subdirectory structure that mirrors zone boundaries.

The package has no Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) referenced in any zone analysis, yet the adapter registry pattern in persistence-sync-layer, the gateway module pattern in CLAUDE.md, and the public.ts API surface convention are all non-obvious architectural choices with compounding benefits. Without ADRs, each of these patterns is at risk of being 'simplified away' by contributors who encounter them without understanding their role in the coupling topology.

Three source zones (recommendation-pipeline 0.48/0.52, prd-analysis-engine 0.52/0.48, cli-interface-layer 0.51/0.49) all have cohesion and coupling values within ±0.04 of 0.50. Values this close to the partition midpoint indicate the community detector found no strong natural boundary and placed files arbitrarily. Architectural decisions based on these three zones as distinct boundaries may be invalid — a manual boundary review using call-graph community membership rather than import-graph proximity is needed before investing in zone-level refactoring.

The package has no documented architectural decision records (ADRs). At least three implicit conventions have significant blast radius: (1) CLI output is the sole progress sink for domain logic, (2) cross-package imports must flow through gateway modules, (3) production code must not import test infrastructure. The first and third are currently violated. Capturing these as ADRs would make violations detectable in code review before they accumulate.

store → unit-analyze (20) and store → cli (5) mean the persistence layer has 25 cross-zone imports into domain and presentation layers. A persistence store that imports from the analysis engine and CLI cannot be tested or replaced without the full application stack. Inverting these dependencies — passing formatting callbacks or analysis results into the store rather than having the store import them — would make the store zone a true infrastructure sink with zero outbound domain dependencies.

Establish a minimum zone size of 8 files in Louvain configuration. All three zones below cohesion 0.4 have ≤16 files (two have exactly 4). Small zones produce unreliable cohesion scores due to insufficient internal edge density, generating false-positive fragmentation alerts that obscure genuine architectural risks in larger zones.

The rex package has no src/ directory whose contents map 1:1 to a single zone. Introduce subdirectory structure (e.g. src/core/token-usage/, src/core/domain/) so that filesystem navigation and zone boundary navigation converge. This eliminates the dual mental model burden and makes zone membership discoverable without tooling.

CLAUDE.md mandates src/public.ts as the public API gate for all packages, but no zone analysis references this file as an active enforcement boundary in rex. Add a CI check that rejects any cross-zone import in rex that does not route through the importing zone's public.ts. This converts the CLAUDE.md convention into a mechanical constraint and prevents the prd-domain-operations 23-entry-point problem from worsening.

The rex package lacks any Architecture Decision Records despite containing at least three non-obvious architectural patterns: adapter registry (persistence-sync-layer), gateway module (CLAUDE.md), and public.ts API gate. Create ADRs for each, explicitly documenting the coupling problem each pattern solves and the metric evidence that validates it. Without this documentation, each pattern is a refactoring target for contributors unfamiliar with the zone health history.

chunked-review-state.ts introduces stateful multi-turn session management directly into the CLI command layer. Session state held in CLI command modules cannot be accessed by the MCP server (src/cli/mcp.ts) or any programmatic caller — if chunked review is ever needed in non-interactive contexts, the state management layer must be duplicated. Extract session state into the store zone to make it transport-agnostic.

src/cli/commands/constants.ts has a 6-zone import fan-in but is classified inside cli-interface-layer. Its presence there forces every consumer zone to take a compile-time dependency on the CLI package boundary even when only needing a constant value. Moving it to a standalone shared-constants module would reduce cli-interface-layer's artificial import fan-out and decouple constants from CLI volatility.

store → cli: 5 imports indicate the persistence layer contains CLI-aware logic (likely output formatting or error messages). This is the same domain→CLI inversion found in prd-analysis-engine, confirming it is a package-wide pattern rather than an isolated case. A single shared output-primitives module extracted from cli-interface-layer would eliminate both inversion sites without restructuring either zone.

This converts the CLAUDE.md convention into a mechanical constraint and prevents the prd-domain-operations 23-entry-point problem from worsening.

store → unit-analyze (20) and store → cli (5) mean the persistence layer has 25 cross-zone imports into domain and presentation layers. A persistence store that imports from the analysis engine and CLI cannot be tested or replaced without the full application stack. Inverting these dependencies — passing formatting callbacks or analysis results into the store rather than having the store import them — would make the store zone a true infrastructure sink with zero outbound domain dependencies.
Hub function: walkTree in src/core/tree.ts is called from 29 files (high fan-in expected for this module type) — changes here have wide impact, consider if responsibilities can be narrowed
2 zones exceed architectural risk thresholds (cohesion < 0.4, coupling > 0.6): cli-e2e-test-suite, token-usage-analytics — mandatory refactoring recommended before further development
Zone "persistence-sync-layer" has files across 8 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Document the adapter registry pattern as an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) capturing why it achieves zero circular dependency participation despite spanning 8 directories. This prevents future contributors from 'simplifying' the registry away without understanding its architectural role as the package's only proven coupling-containment mechanism.
Zone "prd-analysis-engine" has files across 10 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The zone is named 'prd-analysis-engine' but its files live under src/analyze/ — the directory name and zone name are inconsistent. All consumers and contributors must translate between the two names, increasing cognitive load. Either rename the zone to match the directory ('analyze') or move the files to src/prd-analysis/ to match the zone name.

prd-analysis-engine imports 12 times from the 'unit' test zone (unit-analyze → unit: 12 in cross-zone table). Production modules must not import test infrastructure — audit and extract any shared fixtures or helpers into a dedicated non-test utility module. This is a runtime correctness risk if test-only modules have side effects or conditional logic.
Zone "recommendation-pipeline" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

recommendation-pipeline has the worst cohesion/coupling ratio in the package (0.48/0.52) — both metrics simultaneously at their worst. This zone provides no isolation benefit while adding routing overhead for every change. Flag as fragile and prioritize absorption into prd-analysis-engine with a clear subdirectory boundary before any adjacent refactor touches shared types.

src/analyze/acknowledge.ts and src/analyze/scanners.ts have names describing analysis concerns but are classified in recommendation-pipeline — rename or relocate to eliminate the semantic mismatch that misleads zone boundary interpretation.

All three detected circular dependency chains pass through the prd-analysis-engine zone; breaking any cycle requires restructuring this zone's imports, making it the single highest-priority refactoring target in the package.

No zone in the source layer acts as a true stable dependency sink: every zone has higher outbound call volume than inbound. A layered architecture requires at least one foundation zone that only receives calls; the absence of such a zone means the entire graph is a peer mesh with no enforced dependency direction.

The data-only zones (rex-project-state, monorepo-root) achieve perfect cohesion and zero coupling, confirming the tooling can identify clean boundaries. The bidirectional coupling in source zones reflects architectural choices that can be reversed — this is a refactoring problem, not a measurement artifact.

The fix-command-module and token-usage-analytics micro-zones (4 files each) contribute to zone fragmentation without providing meaningful isolation — consolidating them into their dominant neighbor zones would reduce total zone count, raise average cohesion, and simplify the dependency graph without losing any functionality.

prd-analysis-engine acts as a coupling hub with bidirectional ties to every other major zone (cli, core, store, unit, token-usage); any interface change in this zone propagates to the entire package — it requires a versioned public contract and strict change-review discipline.

Test infrastructure (unit zone, 4 files) imports 22 times from prd-analysis-engine internals, coupling test helpers to the largest and most unstable zone. Any decomposition of prd-analysis-engine will require coordinated updates to test utilities, raising the effective cost of splitting that zone.

The call graph shows 214 edges from prd-analysis-engine into cli-interface-layer — the analysis engine is calling CLI output/formatting functions directly, embedding presentation logic inside domain analysis code; this is a leaky abstraction that should be resolved by moving all output formatting into cli and having analysis return plain data structures.
Tightly coupled modules: src/cli/commands/prune.ts and src/cli/output.ts — 60 cross-file calls (unidirectional). Consider extracting shared interface or merging

Import-cohesion and call-graph cohesion are inversely correlated across all five analyzed zones, indicating zone boundaries were optimized for import-graph topology rather than runtime call behavior. Using import-proximity alone as the zone boundary criterion will consistently split functionally cohesive modules (high call-graph cohesion) while grouping functionally unrelated files that share imports. A hybrid boundary criterion weighting call-graph community membership more heavily would produce more actionable and stable zone definitions.

No DTO or value-object boundary exists between zones: all cross-zone communication uses rich internal domain objects directly. Any field-level change to a domain type immediately becomes a breaking change across all 300+ cross-zone import edges simultaneously. A single transfer-types module as the sole cross-zone currency would scope the blast radius of type changes to one file.

The MCP server entry point (src/cli/mcp.ts) is co-located with interactive CLI commands in cli-interface-layer. MCP is a stable programmatic API consumed by external tooling; CLI commands are volatile interactive utilities. Co-location means MCP stability is coupled to CLI churn — a CLI refactor can silently break MCP consumers. The MCP transport layer should be an independent zone with its own versioning discipline.

The package has no zone with both zero circular dependency participation AND significant incoming call volume except persistence-sync-layer, which achieves this at the cost of symmetric bilateral coupling. The absence of any unambiguously downstream-only foundation zone means every refactoring in the package carries bilateral blast radius — there is no safe base layer to refactor from the bottom up. Establishing prd-domain-operations as a true sink (by removing its prd-analysis-engine dependency) is a prerequisite for any systematic decomposition of the bidirectionally-coupled zones.

src/cli/commands/constants.ts and src/cli/output.ts function as global shared infrastructure but are classified inside the CLI commands directory. Their 6-zone import fan-in forces every domain zone to take a transitive dependency on the CLI package. Extracting these files into a dedicated shared-types or shared-infrastructure zone would reduce the import fan-in of cli-interface-layer and eliminate several of the bidirectional coupling pairs.
God function: main in src/cli/index.ts calls 48 unique functions — consider decomposing into smaller, focused functions

Add a CI lint rule that fails if any file matching src/**/* is classified by zone analysis into a zone whose ID contains 'test', 'e2e', or 'spec'. This mechanically prevents recurrence of the src/schema/validate.ts misclassification without requiring manual zone audits.

The call-graph cohesion of 1.0 for cli-e2e-test-suite is a false positive: it reflects zero outgoing calls FROM the zone, but src/schema/validate.ts inside the zone CAN be imported by production code, creating a hidden src→test dependency not captured in the call graph. Zone health metrics for zones containing mixed src/ and test/ files should be flagged as unreliable until the production files are relocated.
Zone "CLI End-to-End Test Suite" (cli-e2e-test-suite) has catastrophic risk (score: 0.71, cohesion: 0.29, coupling: 0.71) — requires immediate architectural intervention

136 internal calls in a 4-file zone with 1 incoming edge is a gold-plating risk: if the sole incoming edge is the CLI router, the entire implementation surface is unreachable as a library. Add at least one direct programmatic test of the core fix logic bypassing the CLI boundary before the zone grows further.

Rename zone from 'fix-command-module' to 'fix-command' to align with the domain/responsibility naming convention used by all other zones; the '-module' suffix is a structural term that adds no semantic information and breaks the implicit taxonomy.

23 entry points with 128 incoming call-graph edges and no visible public.ts gate means callers attach to arbitrary internal files. Enforce the src/public.ts contract by adding a CI lint rule that rejects cross-zone imports not routed through public.ts — this converts 128 uncontrolled attachment points into a single versioned surface.

src/core/analytics.ts belongs to a different zone than src/core/token-usage.ts despite sharing the same directory and conceptual domain. Either relocate analytics.ts into token-usage-analytics or explicitly document in CLAUDE.md that analytics.ts is a general-purpose utility distinct from token-usage calculations to prevent future misclassification.

Configure a minimum zone size floor of 8 files in the Louvain parameters to prevent micro-zone creation. Both 4-file zones (token-usage-analytics cohesion 0.29, fix-command-module cohesion 0.4) fall below the measurement reliability threshold for import-cohesion, generating misleading low-cohesion alerts for zones that are functionally self-contained (call-graph cohesion 0.92 and 0.9 respectively).

token-usage-analytics has a regression test but no interface contract: 42 incoming callers are protected against previously-known bugs but not against future interface changes. Define an explicit TypeScript interface for token-usage inputs and outputs in a shared types file so that any breaking change becomes a compile error across all 42 callers rather than a runtime regression.
Zone "Token Usage Analytics" (token-usage-analytics) has catastrophic risk (score: 0.71, cohesion: 0.29, coupling: 0.71) — requires immediate architectural intervention
